title_en: Taharah: Purification, Water, Wudu, and Ghusl
content_en:
Taharah (purification) is the foundation of worship in Islam. The Prophet (peace be upon him) said: 'Cleanliness is half of faith' (Muslim). In Hanbali fiqh as presented in Zad al-Mustaqni', taharah encompasses ritual purity (tahara shar'iyyah) achieved through wudu, ghusl, and tayammum, as well as physical cleanliness (tahara hissiyyah) by removing najasah (impurities).

Water is categorized in Hanbali fiqh as: (1) Mutlaq — pure and purifying, which is water in its natural state; (2) Water mixed with a pure substance that changes its description and removes the label 'water'; (3) Used water (musta'mal) from a minor ritual washing — pure itself but not purifying according to the stronger opinion in the madhab; (4) Najis water — impure due to contact with a physical impurity that has altered its taste, color, or smell.

The obligatory acts (faraid) of wudu are six: washing the face (including rinsing the mouth and nostrils), washing the arms up to and including the elbows, wiping the entire head (including the ears), washing the feet to and including the ankles, maintaining proper sequence (tartib), and continuity (muwalah). This is based on the Quranic verse: 'O you who believe, when you rise for prayer, wash your faces, your hands and arms to the elbows, wipe your heads, and wash your feet to the ankles' (5:6).

The nullifiers of wudu include: anything exiting from the two passages (front and back), including urine, feces, wind, and other discharges; loss of consciousness or deep sleep; touching the private parts directly without a barrier; and eating camel meat — a distinctive Hanbali ruling based on the hadith narrated in Sahih Muslim. Al-Hajjawi includes camel meat among the nullifiers following the preponderant Hanbali opinion.

Ghusl (major ritual bath) becomes obligatory upon: emission of semen with pleasure, sexual intercourse even without ejaculation, cessation of menstruation (hayd), cessation of postnatal bleeding (nifas), and death (upon other Muslims). The Prophet (peace be upon him) said: 'Water is due to water' (Muslim), meaning ghusl is required when semen is emitted.

The obligatory acts of ghusl are two: intention (niyyah) and washing the entire body including the hair roots. The Prophet's method of ghusl, as described in Sahih al-Bukhari and Muslim by 'A'ishah (may Allah be pleased with her), included washing the hands, removing impurity if present, performing wudu, pouring water over the head three times, and then washing the entire body.

Tayammum is permitted when water is unavailable or when using water would cause harm. One strikes clean earth or dust with the palms, wipes the face, then wipes both hands to the wrists. Tayammum substitutes for both wudu and ghusl when the conditions for its validity are present.
